key_insight: A reduced-basis hybrid TBNN/UDE framework can learn frame-invariant viscoelastic constitutive laws from shear-flow data and transfer them to more complex flows, including extensional and benchmark CFD cases.
engineering_relevance: This provides a path to data-efficient digital twins for viscoelastic fluids that can be embedded directly in finite-volume solvers for computational rheology and CFD.
method: Combine Tensor Basis Neural Networks with Universal Differential Equations to learn constitutive response.
Use a reduced-dimensional tensor basis to improve physical consistency and numerical stability.
Embed the learned model into an open-source finite-volume solver for runtime stress prediction.
evidence_signal: The models were trained on synthetic oscillatory-shear datasets from established viscoelastic laws and then evaluated under extrapolation to unseen extensional flows, 2D/3D benchmark problems, and higher Deborah numbers.
watch_item: Performance drops as extrapolation increases, so models trained only on shear data may need additional stress information or broader training coverage for reliable deployment.
paper_title: Harnessing Machine Learning for Hybrid Constitutive Modelling of Viscoelastic Fluid Flows in Computational Rheology
